claim: Increasing from one camera to two cameras at a single survey site produces a 22–400% increase in probability of detecting a mammal species present at the site, with the largest gains for species with low single-camera detection probability.
stance_target: two-camera deployment as minimum standard for lost dog detection
scope_conditions: six North American mammal species in Maine forests; January–March 2017; winter conditions; camera arrays with bait; results may vary by season and region
quoted_support: Across all six species, we found substantial increases in probability of detection as the number of cameras increased from one to two (22 to 400 percent increase), regardless of the spacing between cameras.

claim: Adding a third camera to a two-camera site provides additional but diminishing detection gains of 4–85%, with the largest additional gain for coyote — a low-frequency mover analogous to very shy or long-range displaced dogs.
stance_target: diminishing returns from third camera — two cameras is the cost-effective optimum for most scenarios
scope_conditions: same study as above; coyote had the lowest daily availability probability (0.07) and showed the most gain from third camera
quoted_support: For most species the magnitude of the increase was less substantial when adding a third camera (4 to 85 percent increase), with coyote and snowshoe hare showing a pronounced effect.

claim: Camera spacing (100m vs 150m) had less impact on detection probability than camera count — adding a second camera at any spacing is more important than optimizing distance between cameras.
stance_target: camera count prioritized over spacing optimization in search design
scope_conditions: spacing range tested was narrow (100m vs 150m); greater spacing differences may show larger effects
quoted_support: the influence of the transect spacing, either 100 m or 150 m, was not as strong as increasing the number of cameras

claim: For coyote (the most shy, low-presence species in the study, analogous to a traumatized fleeing dog), single-camera detection probability per day was only 0.9% (ϴ × p = 0.07 × 0.13), rising to 3.6% with two cameras and 4.6% with three.
stance_target: multiple cameras as essential for detecting very shy or low-frequency subjects
scope_conditions: coyote in winter Maine; daily availability ϴ = 0.07 is extremely low; lost shy dogs may have higher availability at feeding stations

claim: Placing cameras farther from roads/trails improved detection of fisher and marten — some species actively avoid road proximity, suggesting shy/traumatized dogs may be more detectable at cameras placed away from main travel routes.
stance_target: off-road/off-trail camera placement as superior for detecting shy animals
scope_conditions: roads vs snowmobile trails in forested Maine; translates to paved vs dirt tracks in Algarve context
quoted_support: Fisher especially had higher probability of detection for methods located on average farther from the access route, suggesting that researchers should avoid only placing sites close to roadways despite the logistic ease.

claim: Multi-scale occupancy modeling confirms that all six species showed significant detection improvement by camera number — camera count effects were consistent across all trophic levels, body sizes, and movement strategies.
stance_target: camera array approach as generalizable across species/behavioral profiles
scope_conditions: six species spanning carnivores, herbivores, small mammals; winter; Maine boreal forest
quoted_support: In the first stage of multi-scale occupancy modeling, the inclusion of the method type in the detection process always performed better than the null model (ΔAICc > 8 for all species), confirming that the number and spacing of cameras had an important effect on the likelihood of detecting all species.

claim: Using pooled detection histories from multiple cameras at a single site reduces false-absences and improves occupancy estimates — the practical implication is that a single missed capture is far less likely with multi-camera arrays.
stance_target: pooled multi-camera data as more reliable than any single camera for presence confirmation
scope_conditions: any camera trap survey; all ecosystems
quoted_support: increasing the number of camera units at a site has been demonstrated to increase detection probability, the magnitude of the increase is likely to vary between regions and target species... Increasing the number of camera units is a valuable tool and can provide large gains with low added cost when including the time and effort expended to reach each survey site
